title: SEGMENT 16 — Comparing with cerebral organoids: when does complexity become embryo-like?

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Comparing with cerebral organoids: when does complexity become embryo-like?

I want to spend a few minutes on the comparison between synthetic embryos and cerebral organoids, because the relationship between the two is illuminating and because the question of where one ends and the other begins is genuinely fuzzy.
Cerebral organoids — the lab-grown mini-brains we talked about in the organoid lecture — are stem-cell-derived structures that model aspects of early brain development.
They're made from pluripotent stem cells.
They grow in defined media with specific signaling factors.
They self-organize into structures with multiple cell types, ventricle-like cavities, layered cortical organization, even some functional electrical activity in advanced versions.
They've been used for a wide range of research applications — modeling microcephaly, studying Zika virus infection, exploring autism-spectrum disorder genetics, and so on.
Cerebral organoids are clearly not embryos.
They lack the other lineages — no trophoblast, no extraembryonic endoderm.
They don't have the architecture of a whole embryo.
They can never become a complete organism.
They're tissue models, organ models, not embryo models.
Synthetic embryos, conversely, clearly mimic embryos.
They have multiple lineages including the extraembryonic ones.
They have the architecture of an embryo.
They can, in principle and in the most advanced versions, develop into organisms (in mouse, at least up to day 8.5).
Between these two clearly distinct categories — clear non-embryo and clear embryo-mimic — there's a fuzzy middle, and that fuzzy middle is where the most interesting and difficult cases live.
Here are some examples that sit in the fuzzy middle.
Gastruloids.
These are stem-cell-derived structures that, in defined conditions, recapitulate aspects of gastrulation — the establishment of the three germ layers and the early body axis.
They're made from pluripotent cells alone, without trophoblast or extraembryonic endoderm.
They look like elongating, axially organized clumps with mesoderm, endoderm, and ectoderm-like domains.
They model early body plan formation, particularly the events that happen during the third week of human development.

They have less of an embryo's overall organization — no clear head fold, no trophoblast, no extraembryonic structures — but they do recapitulate one of the key events that the 14-day rule was protecting.
Are gastruloids embryos?
Probably not, by most reasonable definitions.
But they engage some of the same ethical concerns.
Trunk-like structures and somitoids.
These are stem-cell-derived structures that model the formation of the trunk and the segmentation of mesoderm into somites.
They're more developmentally specialized than gastruloids — they focus on a particular subset of body plan formation.
They're clearly not embryos.
But they're modeling embryonic events.
Heart organoids that beat.
These are stem-cell-derived cardiac structures that recapitulate aspects of early heart development, sometimes with spontaneous beating activity.
They're clearly not embryos.
But they're modeling a key embryonic organ.
Cerebral organoids with extended development.
Advanced cerebral organoids, grown for many months, develop substantial complexity — multiple cortical layers, neural circuitry, oscillating electrical activity.
They're still clearly not embryos — no other germ layers — but they're modeling a major embryonic organ to a degree of fidelity that increases over time.
What this shows is that the synthetic-embryo concept isn't quite cleanly separable from a broader category of "stem cell models of embryonic events.
" Cerebral organoids are clearly on one end; integrated embryo models like the Hanna 2023 work are clearly on the other; gastruloids and other partial embryo models sit somewhere in between.
The ISSCR guidelines try to address this by distinguishing "integrated embryo models" from "non-integrated embryo models.
" An integrated model has all the major lineages of the early embryo (epiblast, trophoblast, extraembryonic endoderm) and is recapitulating the integrated architecture of an embryo.
A non-integrated model has some but not all of the lineages, or models only specific embryonic events without the full architecture.
Integrated models are recommended for stricter oversight; non-integrated models are recommended for somewhat more relaxed oversight.
This distinction is reasonable, but it doesn't fully dissolve the fuzziness.

A particularly advanced non-integrated model — a gastruloid that goes through extensive axial organization, say — could plausibly engage more ethical concern than a relatively primitive integrated model.
The category lines are useful starting points but they're not bright bright lines.
There's a deeper point here.
The distinction between organoids and synthetic embryos is, in some ways, a distinction of degree rather than kind.
Both involve stem cells.
Both involve self-organization.
Both recapitulate aspects of normal development.
They differ in scope — synthetic embryos try to model whole embryos, organoids try to model individual organs — but the underlying biology is continuous, not categorically distinct.
This matters because it means the ethical concerns about synthetic embryos are not entirely separable from the ethical concerns about advanced organoids.

A cerebral organoid grown to such complexity that it has substantial neural circuitry and possibly even some kind of integrated activity is not a synthetic embryo, but it engages questions about consciousness and moral status that are at least adjacent to embryo ethics.

As organoid technology and synthetic embryo technology both advance, the categories may need to be reorganized around the underlying biology rather than the historical accidents of how the work developed.
The two-axis framework I introduced in Segment 14 — developmental progress and morphological similarity — is one way to think about this unification.

Cerebral organoids are high on one kind of fidelity (the fidelity of the modeled organ) but low on integrated developmental progress (they don't recapitulate the whole embryo's developmental trajectory).
Synthetic embryos are higher on integrated developmental progress but their fidelity is more diffused across multiple lineages.
Both have ethical implications.
The implications differ in detail but overlap in kind.

The field is, I think, slowly converging on a more unified framework that treats organoids and synthetic embryos as related rather than distinct ethical categories, with concerns calibrated to the specific features of each construct rather than to which historical category it belongs in.
That's the direction the ISSCR guidelines have been moving, and I expect it's the direction national regulations will increasingly take as well.
